---
title: "Built on Stan (and R):  `blavaan` and `brms`"
author: "Mitzi Morris"
institute: "Stan Development Team"
date: June 22, 2026
format:
  beamer:
    highlight-style: tango
    aspectratio: 169
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
    theme: metropolis
    include-in-header: preamble.tex
    navigation: horizontal
    dev: png
    colorlinks: true
---

# The Interface You Know + Stan and Workflow Tools

\vspace{0.075in}

\tightlist
* `blavaan`: Bayesian Structural Equation Modeling in Stan      
Fits latent variable models, including confirmatory factor analysis,         
structural equation models, and latent growth curve models.     
SEM Syntax (like `lavaan`, LISREL)       
`blavaan` homepage: [https://blavaan.org/](https://blavaan.org/)      

\vspace{0.075in}

* `brms`:  Bayesian Regression and Multilevelmodeling in Stan           
Fit Bayesian generalized (non-)linear multivariate multilevel models.       
R formula syntax (extended).      
BRMS homepage: [https://paul-buerkner.github.io/brms/](https://paul-buerkner.github.io/brms/)

\vspace{0.075in}

* Workflow tools     
Stan sampler diagnostics, draws + R packages [`posterior`](https://mc-stan.org/posterior/), [`loo`](https://mc-stan.org/loo/), [`bayesplot`](https://mc-stan.org/bayesplot/)
Python ecosystem:  [ArviZ](https://python.arviz.org/en/stable/)



# Bayesian Workflow (elements of)
\vspace{-0.15in}

* Exploratory data analysis / Data elicitation
* Model specification
* Model checking (simulated data)
  + Recover parameters
  + Prior predictive distribution checking
  + Calibration checking
* Model checking (simulated or observed data)
  + Sample diagnostics
  + Posterior predictive checks
* Model comparison
  + Leave-one out cross-validation



# The Need for High-level Interfaces

::: {.quote-block}
> "Despite the increased popularity of Bayesian latent variable models, **coding the models via JAGS (Plummer 2003) or Stan (Carpenter et al. 2017) syntax can be difficult, and the resulting sampling can be time-consuming and inefficient**.

>

> Other R packages have addressed these issues for related models, including \texttt{brms} (Bürkner 2017) for mixed and multivariate models, \texttt{rstanarm} (Gabry and Goodrich 2020) for regression models, \texttt{ctsem} (Driver et al. 2017) for time series models, \texttt{edstan} (Furr 2017) for item response models, and \texttt{pcFactorStan} (Pritikin 2021) for pairwise comparison factor models."

- **Merkle et al**, "Efficient Bayesian Structural Equation Modeling in Stan"
:::


# `blavaan`: like `lavaan`, only Bayesian

The R package [`lavaan`](https://lavaan.ugent.be/) provides a high-level syntax for specifying and fitting latent variable models
using maximum likelihood estimation by default.

The [`blavaan`](https://blavaan.org) package uses this syntax and produces full Bayesian posterior estimates.

\fontsize{9.5pt}{10.5}\selectfont
```r
model <- '
  # measurement model
  math =~ PV1MATH1 + PV1MATH2 + PV1MATH3 + PV1MATH4
  neg.efficacy =~ ST31Q01 + ST31Q02 + ST31Q03 + ST31Q04
  neg.selfconcept =~ ST32Q04 + ST32Q06 + ST32Q07 + ST32Q09
  # structural regression
  math ~ neg.selfconcept + neg.efficacy
'
fit  <- lavaan::sem(model, data = pisa.be.2003)
bfit <- blavaan::bsem(model, data = pisa.be.2003)
```
\normalsize



# Merkle et al, 2021 `blavaan` 

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\href{https://www.jstatsoft.org/article/view/v100i06}{\includegraphics[height=0.2\textheight, left]{img/jss_header.jpg}}
\includegraphics[height=0.42\textheight, left]{img/jss_title.jpg}
\captionsetup{skip=1pt, justification=raggedright, singlelinecheck=false}
\end{figure}

\vspace{0.1in}

::: {.quote-block}
> "The primary contribution of this paper is to describe and illustrate a new approach to Stan SEM estimation, which greatly improves the speed and efficiency of model."
:::

# Structural Equation Models

For observation $i$, a structural equation model with $p$ observed variables
$y_i$ and $m$ latent variables $\eta_i$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_i   &= \nu + \Lambda \eta_i + \epsilon_i \\
\eta_i &= \alpha + \mathrm{B}\,\eta_i + \zeta_i
\end{aligned}
$$

with residuals $\epsilon_i \sim N_p(0, \Theta)$ and $\zeta_i \sim N_m(0, \Psi)$.

* $\Lambda$ — factor loadings ($p \times m$)
* $\mathrm{B}$ — regressions among latent variables ($m \times m$)
* $\Theta$ — covariance of measurement errors $\epsilon_i$ ($p \times p$)
* $\Psi$ — covariance of structural errors $\zeta_i$ ($m \times m$)
* $\nu, \alpha$ — intercepts for observed and latent variables


# `blavaan` - JAGS Model (2018)

* The usual Bayesian approach samples two sets of parameters:
   + the structural model parameters:  $\Lambda$, $\mathrm{B}$, $\Psi$, $\Theta$
   + the $\mathrm{N} \times \mathrm{m}$ matrix of latent variables, where $\mathrm{N}$ is the number of observations (range of $\mathrm{i}$).

* As $\mathrm{N}$ increases, the parameter space grows and the sampler becomes slower and less efficient (latent scores tangle with the variance parameters).



# `blavaan` New Stan Approach (2021)

\vspace{0.2in}

::: {.quote-block}
>"The key to fast sampling in Stan is to work with a model likelihood that is marginal over latent variables"
:::

\tightlist
* Integrate out the latent variables. In the Gaussian case there is a closed form: $y_i$ is multivariate normal with
  $$\Sigma = \Lambda(\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{B})^{-1}\Psi(\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{B})^{-\top}\Lambda^\top + \Theta$$

* Sample only the fixed structural parameters $\Lambda$, $\mathrm{B}$, $\Psi$, $\Theta$

* Factor scores, if needed, are recovered afterward in `generated quantities`.

* \textit{Caveat:} the closed form depends on Gaussian conjugacy, so it does not apply directly to non-normal observed variables.




# `blavaan` Efficiency - Political Democracy Bollen (1989)

\begincols
\begincol{0.6\linewidth}        
  \vspace{0.15in}
  \includegraphics[width=0.98\linewidth]{img/blavaan_ess.jpg}
  Effective sample size per 100 iterations
\endcol
\begincol{0.4\linewidth}        
\fontsize{9pt}{10}\selectfont
```
model <- '
   # measurement model
     ind60 =~ x1 + x2 + x3
     dem60 =~ y1 + y2 + y3 + y4
     dem65 =~ y5 + y6 + y7 + y8
   # regressions
     dem60 ~ ind60
     dem65 ~ ind60 + dem60
   # residual covariances
     y1 ~~ y5
     y2 ~~ y4 + y6
     y3 ~~ y7
     y4 ~~ y8
     y6 ~~ y8
'
```
\normalsize

\endcol
\endcols

# `blavaan`: Same SEM, Different Estimator

\tightlist
* `lavaan`: familiar SEM syntax, maximum likelihood estimation.
* `blavaan`: same syntax, Bayesian estimation in Stan.

* When data are large and priors are weak, estimates can look similar.
* The practical difference is what else the fitted model gives you:
   + posterior uncertainty, not just asymptotic standard errors
   + explicit priors and prior sensitivity
   + MCMC diagnostics
   + Bayesian fit indices and posterior predictive checks


\vspace{0.1in}

* Stan Case study: [Bayesian Structural Equation Modeling using `blavaan`](https://mc-stan.org/learn-stan/case-studies/sem.html)
   + Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA), Latent Growth Curve Models, SEM Models, Bayesian Model Evaluation

* Google Group:  [https://groups.google.com/g/blavaan](https://groups.google.com/g/blavaan)

* Ed Merkle case studies:  [https://ecmerkle.org/](https://ecmerkle.org/)



# BRMS:  Bayesian Regression and Multilevelmodeling in Stan
\vspace{-0.1in}
[BRMS package](https://paul-buerkner.github.io/brms/) function `brm` fits multilevel models

\tightlist
* model specified using extended R formula syntax
* rich set of distributions
* workflow checks built-in

\vspace{0.1in}

\fontsize{10pt}{11}\selectfont
\begincols
\begincol{0.1\linewidth}        
\endcol
\begincol{0.9\linewidth}        
```
# model with treatment effect
fit1 <- brm(count ~ zAge + zBase + Trt + ( 1 | patient) + ( 1 | obs),
            data = epilepsy, family = negbinomial(),
            prior = prior(normal(0, 1), class = b),
            seed = 12345, backend = "cmdstanr")
```
\endcol
\endcols
\normalsize

\vspace{-0.1in}
\begincols
\begincol{0.25\linewidth}        
  \includegraphics[width=0.55\linewidth, left]{img/brms.png}
\endcol
\begincol{0.75\linewidth}        
 [https://paul-buerkner.github.io/brms/](https://paul-buerkner.github.io/brms/)
\endcol
\endcols

# BRMS processing
\vspace{-0.2in}

Arguments to the `brm` function provide the full specification of the model.

\begincols
\begincol{0.95\linewidth}        
\fontsize{10pt}{11}\selectfont
```
fit1 <- brm(Reaction ~ Days + (Days|Subject),
            data = sleepstudy)
```
\normalsize
\endcol
\endcols

\vspace{0.1in}
From these arguments, BRMS

\tightlist
* generates the Stan code and input data (use functions `stancode` and `standata` to inspect)

* runs CmdStanR to generate a sample

* returns/manages the fitted model object

* fitted model object has methods for plotting, and prediction, model checking, model comparison,



# Multi-level Modeling with BRMS
\vspace{-0.1in}

The conceptual structure of a multi-level model is: $y_i \sim \mathrm{D}(\, {\mathit{f\,}^{-1}}(\eta_i),\,\theta )$

\tightlist
* $\mathrm{y}$ is the modeled data, i.e., the observed outcome generated by the model given the (unmodeled) inputs
* $\mathrm{D}$ is a distributional **family** (default gaussian)
* $\eta$ is the linear predictor 
   + $\eta$ can be transformed by an inverse _link function_ $\mathit{f}$
   + $\eta$ can be rewritten as $\mathrm{X}\,\beta + \mathrm{Z\,u}$ where             
     $\beta$ and $\mathrm{u}$ are population-level and group-level coefficients respectively and                   
     $\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Z}$ are the corresponding design matrices      
* $\theta$ denotes family-specific parameter(s)


# Model Specification in BRMS

\tightlist
* The extended formula syntax for a multilevel model has the general form          
  \texttt{response $\sim$ pterms + (gterms | group)}           
  where:        

   + pterms: the population-level effects, constant across observations.
   + gterms: the group-level effects, varying across the levels of a group.
   + the intercept term is either $1$ or $0$ for no intercept; if unspecified, default is $1$.

\vspace{0.075in}

* Arguments to the `brm` function provide the full specification of the model.
  + first argument:  the extended-syntax  R formula for $\eta$, the linear predictor
  + second argument (`data`) - a dataframe containing all data for $y$, $X$, and $Z$.
  + arg `family` - distributional family $\mathrm{D}$ (can define custom family in Stan)
  + arg `prior` - priors on all model parameters
  + (advanced options)


# BRMS Processing
* Pass all information to BRMS

\fontsize{9pt}{9pt}\selectfont
```r
brm(Reaction ~ Days + (Days|Subject), data = sleepstudy)
```
\normalsize

* Get results

\fontsize{9pt}{9pt}\selectfont
```r
 Family: gaussian 
  Links: mu = identity; sigma = identity 
Formula: Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject) 
   Data: sleepstudy (Number of observations: 180) 

Multilevel Hyperparameters:    		     	  Regression Coefficients:
~Subject (Number of levels: 18) 		            Estimate Est.Error
                    Estimate Est.Error		  Intercept   251.31      7.40
sd(Intercept)          27.27      7.03		  Days         10.45      1.70
sd(Days)                6.60      1.51		  sigma        25.87      1.52
cor(Intercept,Days)     0.07      0.30
```
\normalsize


# BRMS Priors

[Prior Definitions for brms Models](https://paulbuerkner.com/brms/reference/set_prior.html)
\fontsize{9pt}{9.1pt}\selectfont
```r
> prior_summary(sleep_mlm_brmsfit)
                     prior     class      coef   group resp dpar nlpar lb ub       source
                    (flat)         b                                              default
                    (flat)         b      Days                               (vectorized)
 student_t(3, 288.7, 59.3) Intercept                                              default
      lkj_corr_cholesky(1)         L                                              default
      lkj_corr_cholesky(1)         L           Subject                       (vectorized)
     student_t(3, 0, 59.3)        sd                                    0         default
     student_t(3, 0, 59.3)        sd           Subject                  0    (vectorized)
     student_t(3, 0, 59.3)        sd      Days Subject                  0    (vectorized)
     student_t(3, 0, 59.3)        sd Intercept Subject                  0    (vectorized)
     student_t(3, 0, 59.3)     sigma                                    0         default
```
\normalsize



# Reasons to Use BRMS

\tightlist
* Familiar syntax (for R users).

\vspace{0.05in}
* Handles many common models:  IRT, zero-inflated, additive distributional, phylogenetic, missing data imputation.
  + makes varying slope/ varying intercept models easy.

\vspace{0.05in}
* Generated models are sound and robust
  + zero-centers predictors  (like RStanARM).

\vspace{0.05in}
* Integrated diagnostics and model checks
  + prior and posterior predictive checking
  + leave-one-out cross-validation

\vspace{0.05in}
* Large community of users
  + majority of questions on Stan mailing list are BRMS.
  + many helpful community experts to answer them!

# BRMS Tradeoffs

\begincols
\begincol{0.4\linewidth}        

\vspace{0.15in}
\includegraphics[height=0.9\textheight]{img/brms_processing.png}
\endcol
\begincol{0.6\linewidth}        

* BRMS makes choices:
    + default priors (easy to override).
    + uses the [non-centered parameterization](https://mc-stan.org/docs/stan-users-guide/efficiency-tuning.html#reparameterization.section) for hierarchical models (cannot override).

* Generated Stan code is hard to read or modify.

* No free lunch!
    + complicated models require complicated specifications.

\endcol
\endcols





# Bayesian Workflow

* The new book:  [Bayesian Workflow](https://avehtari.github.io/Bayesian-Workflow/)

* Section 5.2 of Merkle et al 2021:  Simulation-Based Calibration to investigate priors.

* Key paper:

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\href{https://academic.oup.com/jrsssa/article/182/2/389/7070184}{%
  \includegraphics[height=0.32\textheight, left]{img/RSS_Gabry_2019.png}
}
\end{figure}

# Running example

\tightlist

\vspace{0.1in}
\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\includegraphics[height=0.32\textheight]{img/shaddick_2016.png}
\captionsetup{skip=0pt, justification=raggedright, singlelinecheck=false}
\caption*{\fontsize{10pt}{11}\selectfont \ \ \textit{https://arxiv.org/pdf/1609.00141.pdf}}
\end{figure}

* Air pollution from particles less than 2.5 microns in diameter (${PM}_{2.5}$).
* $107$ countries, $7$ WHO regions, $6003$ paired satellite/ground monitor observations at $2980$ locations


# Shaddick et al - Ground-level air pollution

\vspace{-0.2in}
\tightlist

\vspace{0.05in}
\begincols[T]
\begincol{0.33\linewidth}
\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\includegraphics[height=0.32\textheight]{img/shaddick_fig_1.png}
\captionsetup{skip=1pt}
\caption*{\fontsize{9pt}{10}\selectfont ${PM}_{2.5}$ ground monitor readings}
\end{figure}
\endcol
\begincol{0.33\linewidth}
\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\includegraphics[height=0.32\textheight]{img/shaddick_fig_2.png}
\captionsetup{skip=1pt}
\caption*{\fontsize{9pt}{10}\selectfont ${PM}_{2.5}$ satellite readings}
\end{figure}
\endcol
\begincol{0.33\linewidth}
\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\includegraphics[height=0.32\textheight]{img/shaddick_fig_4.png}
\captionsetup{skip=1pt}
\caption*{\fontsize{9pt}{10}\selectfont Population density}
\end{figure}
\endcol
\endcols

\tightlist
* Ground monitors: accurate but sparse (no data for $70$ countries),                 
  cluster near (some) populations.
* Satellites: global coverage but noisier (optical artifacts).
* Shaddick et al.'s broader goal: use satellite coverage to estimate PM2.5.
* Gabry et al. use the data to illustrate the Bayesian workflow by building a small network of models that relate ${PM}_{2.5}$ ground monitor readings to co-located satellite readings.

# Workflow: Exploratory Data Analysis

\fontsize{13pt}{15}\selectfont
_The Model Can Often Only Be Understood in the Context of the Data_
\normalsize

* The model describes the data generating process
* Data tendencies require different priors
* Large data / small data regimes require centered / non-centered parameterization

[The Prior Can Often Only Be Understood in the Context of the Likelihood](https://www.mdpi.com/1099-4300/19/10/555)

\hfill Gelman et al, 2017

# EDA: What Patterns are in the Data?

\begincols
\begincol{0.25\linewidth}        

* Outcome: ground monitor $\log(PM_{2.5})$
* (Predictor: satellite $\log(PM_{2.5})$)
* What are the ground monitor readings?

\endcol
\begincol{0.75\linewidth}        
\includegraphics[height=0.75\textheight]{img/brms_eda_log_pm25_map.png}
\endcol
\endcols

# EDA: Complete Pooling vs. Group Countries?

\begincols
\begincol{0.5\linewidth}        

* Outcome: ground monitor $\log(PM_{2.5})$
* Predictor: satellite $\log(PM_{2.5})$
* Plot colored by WHO super-region, single regression line.

\endcol
\begincol{0.50\linewidth}        
\includegraphics[height=0.75\textheight]{img/gabry_plot1.png}
\endcol
\endcols

# EDA: What Grouping Structure is Plausible?

\begincols
\begincol{0.50\linewidth}        

* Group by WHO super-region
   + 1 region for the rich countries
   + 6 geo-graphical regions
  
* Group by ground monitor readings
   + 7 clusters

\endcol
\begincol{0.50\linewidth}        
\includegraphics[height=0.85\textheight]{img/gabry_fig2.png}
\endcol
\endcols


# EDA: What Grouping Structure is Plausible?
\vspace{0.1in}

\begincols
\begincol{0.5\linewidth}        
\includegraphics[height=0.75\textheight]{img/gabry_plot2.png}

Group by WHO super-region
\endcol

\begincol{0.5\linewidth}        
\includegraphics[height=0.75\textheight]{img/gabry_plot3.png}

Cluster on ground monitor $\log(PM_{2.5})$

\endcol
\endcols

# BRMS Models

\vspace{-0.1in}


1. Complete pooling:
   one calibration curve for all sites.

\fontsize{9.5pt}{10.5}\selectfont
```r
    fit_complete_pool =
      brm(log_pm25 ~ 1 + log_sat,
          data = sites)
```
\normalsize

2. Partial pooling by WHO region:
   region-specific intercepts and slopes.

\fontsize{9.5pt}{10.5}\selectfont
```r
    fit_super_region =
      brm(log_pm25 ~ 1 + log_sat + (1 + log_sat | super_region_name),
          data = sites)
```
\normalsize

3. Partial pooling by pollution cluster:
   cluster-specific intercepts and slopes.

\fontsize{9.5pt}{10.5}\selectfont
```r
    fit_cluster_region =
      brm(log_pm25 ~ 1 + log_sat + (1 + log_sat | cluster_region),
          data = sites)
```
\normalsize


# Gabry et al - Prior predictive checks

\vspace{-0.1in}
Visualize (or summarize) the prior marginal distribution of the data

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\includegraphics[height=0.7\textheight]{img/gabry_fig_4.png}
\end{figure}

Note:  histogram of just the simulated data also informative.


# Model Correctness Checks
\vspace{-0.1in}

Given a single model and data, either collected or simulated,
the most basic correctness checks are:

* Can the sampler fit the data satisfactorily?
  + Grossly misspecified models fail to initialize, or chains fail to converge.

* Did NUTS fit the model without warnings?
* Are $\hat{R}$ and ESS satisfactory?
* Are posterior estimates on a plausible scale?

Difficult to fit models may require reparameterization


# Workflow in Action:  BRMS Notebook

We use Jupyter lab to run the notebook `fit_air_brms.qmd`
